runtimeRecordings.scenario: drop while reflow is still running

verifiedBehavior.fact: DragDropEventType 3 is drop and 4 is drag-leave; both clear the active drag view models.

Rearrangement resolves an anchor-adjusted tile hit point, converts it to group-relative cells, and delegates collision placement to the layout resolver instead of using the raw cursor as the tile origin.
The reflow timer interval is 1200000 100-ns ticks (120 ms). Moving to a new drag point restarts the timer; a stable point emits one reflow and records that point.
The drag engine maintains a placeholder tile and only converts the placeholder model to the final model after the placeholder visual has loaded.
A tile move is transactional: rearrange event 0 begins the move operation; terminal event 2 or 3 ends it, and event 3 is recorded as a successful completion.
Cross-group movement removes the tile from its old host before adding it to the destination host at the resolved cell.

Dragging a tile out of an existing group uses an explicit new-container path. FindTargetGroup returns RearrangeNewContainerLocation before, after, or empty-column; UpdateNewGroupIndicator renders the provisional group boundary; OnTileRearrangeEvent creates the group model and then moves the tile into it.

New-container hit testing first maps the anchor-adjusted horizontal point through GroupColumnFromPixels, sorts candidate groups by ITileGroupViewModel.CellLocation, and compares the vertical hit against HeaderHeight and GroupHeightFromCells midpoint bands. _GetNewGroupLocation resolves before, after, and empty-column results from model cell coordinates rather than animated visual Top positions.

A medium tile detached into the vertical gap between two groups creates a new intervening group. The tile retains the horizontal drop cell within that group rather than compacting to column zero, and the following group moves below it.

A one-cell IntelliJ IDEA displacement measured at 120 FPS fits a 400 ms cubic-bezier(0.1, 0.9, 0.2, 1) reflow. A grid search over 120-500 ms produced its lowest mean squared position error at approximately 399 ms, so the existing recovered theme parameters are retained.
evidence: 120 FPS frames 112-142: tracked IntelliJ IDEA icon center from the current visible position to the next cell

verifiedBehavior.fact: For a settled drop, the composited drag preview is last visible at frame 60 and the destination tile has fully taken over by frame 66. At 120 FPS this is a six-frame, approximately 50 ms visual handoff, not a second long-distance landing translation.
evidence: 120 FPS frames 60-66 in the 6.2-second analysis window

verifiedBehavior.fact: Dropping while a reflow is still active removes the drag preview without restarting affected tiles from their layout origins; the existing reflow continues toward its current destination.

tileStartInternalDropGeometry.behavior: Existing-group and new-group hit testing use one immutable set of outer group bounds and CellLocation coordinates for the duration of an internal tile drag. Reflow animations may move visuals but cannot move the logical hit boundaries until the transaction ends.
tileStartInternalDropGeometry.reason: Using live visual Top values after a provisional group insertion creates a feedback loop: the preview moves the following group, the next hit test changes from the intended row to the following row, and the preview repeatedly recreates itself. StartUI resolves the new-container path from model CellLocation and grid metrics instead of animated visual coordinates.

tileStartReflowMotion.retargeting: A new FLIP animation starts from the element's current composited visible position. The active TranslateTransform offset must be retained when the previous animation is replaced.

tileStartDragPreviewMotion.positionDriver: RenderTransform TranslateTransform; pointer movement must not update Canvas.Left or Canvas.Top
tileStartDragPreviewMotion.cancelReturnStatus: TileStart approximation; no isolated original Esc/cancel recording is available

tileStartDetachmentBoundary.behavior: An internal tile drag snapshots each existing group's height before the rollbackable preview transaction starts. Dragged-tile new-group detection compares against that snapshot, while current Left, Top, Width, and live Height remain available for scrolling, ordinary hit testing, and newly created provisional groups.
tileStartDetachmentBoundary.reason: A preview that moves a tile into a lower supported row can expand TileGroup.PixelHeight. Reusing that live height for detachment makes the group bottom follow the dragged tile and prevents non-final-row tiles from reaching the new-group creation band.

tileStartDragAutoScroll.behavior: Holding an internal tile drag inside the top or bottom edge zone continuously changes the ScrollViewer offset and reruns tile/group hit testing after each rendered scroll frame.

unresolved: The native resolver's exact tie-breaking order when several mixed-size tiles can satisfy the same collision.

The native Windows 10 Start implementation's exact source of the detachment bottom boundary has not been isolated. TileStart snapshots the pre-drag group height because its own rollbackable preview can expand live PixelHeight; this is a regression-backed compatibility strategy, not evidence that StartUI stores the same field.

The exact native placeholder opacity during reflow is not isolated. TileStart retains its independently calibrated value of 0.24; the reflow duration and spline are now runtime-fitted from the 2026-07-20 120 FPS recording.

The exact original cancel/escape return duration and curve are not isolated. TileStart currently uses a clearly marked 200 ms approximation and requires a dedicated original-menu cancel recording before claiming parity.

The exact native Windows 10 Start drag auto-scroll edge size, acceleration curve, and maximum velocity are not yet isolated. TileStart uses one CellPitch as the edge zone and frame-time-based velocity so the behavior remains continuous across 60/120/240 Hz displays.

The private GridLayoutMetrics calls that define the exact new-group creation band are not fully typed in the public PDB. TileStart's internal drag path follows the verified anchor-adjusted tile geometry and now keeps model-like outer hit boundaries stable for the drag transaction, but still uses half the 32-DIP group-header height as a provisional center-point creation band. The exact native entry threshold, exit threshold, and any hysteresis still require runtime tracing or controlled original-menu video measurement. The pointer-only fallback for external WPF drag events retains the older one-CellPitch threshold.
